conj.statement: 1. **Texas** — Largest refugee admissions in 2023 (2,812), but state bans on federal resettlement contracts (TX Executive Order GA-40, 2023), forcing reliance on non-state actors. 2. **Alaska** — Remote geography, extreme climate, and limited infrastructure increase resettlement costs by 200% vs. national average (GAO, 2023). 3. **Arizona** — High asylum caseload (1,420 in 2023), but state-level restrictions on driver’s license access for asylum seekers (HB 2200, 2023), impeding integration. 4. **Louisiana** — Frequent natural disasters (e.g., Hurricane Ida 2021) strain emergency services; 42%
supporting_evidence: Tension*: Urban counties get 92% of resettlement funds despite 61% settling in suburbs/rural areas (HHS, 2023).
Resolution*: Tiered funding formula weighting remote locations (e.g., +25% for >50-mile trips).
Tension*: Asylum seekers wait 180+ days for work authorization, causing poverty in high-cost cities.
Resolution*: State-level work permits during adjudication (CA AB 60 reduced poverty by 29% in 2022).
Action*: $500M over 5 years for mobile services, broadband, and employer tax credits.
Rationale*: OK’s pilot reduced rural refugee unemployment from 68% to 31% (2023).
Action*: Real-time case tracking with privacy safeguards (modeled on MN’s "Integration Dashboard").
Rationale*: NC’s platform cut processing delays by 22% in 2023 (GAO, 2024).
